content_en:
The practice of muhasabah — holding oneself to account — is one of the most practically transformative disciplines in Islamic spiritual life. Al-Nawawi grounds this chapter in the Quranic command: 'O you who believe, fear Allah, and let every soul look to what it has put forward for tomorrow' (Quran 59:18). This verse explicitly commands the believer to engage in prospective self-examination — to look ahead to the Day of Judgment and assess in advance whether their current path of action will produce what they hope for.

The classical Islamic concept of muhasabah, derived from the same Arabic root as 'hisab' (reckoning/account), involves a daily practice of examining one's deeds: what good has one done, what harm has one caused, what obligations have been neglected, and what sins have been committed. The great Companion and second Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab — whose example in this regard was celebrated by all subsequent scholars — said: 'Hold yourselves to account before you are held to account, and weigh your deeds before they are weighed for you, and adorn yourselves for the greatest presentation — the one who accounts for himself today will find his reckoning easy tomorrow.' This statement by Umar is cited by virtually every classical scholar who writes on muhasabah and represents the essential rationale for the practice.

Al-Nawawi includes hadiths that establish the Prophetic basis for this practice. The Prophet ﷺ described the intelligent (al-kayyis) person as 'one who holds his soul to account and works for what comes after death.' The contrast — the 'ajiz (incapable) person — is 'one who follows his soul's desires and then indulges in wishful thinking about Allah.' (Al-Tirmidhi.) This distinction cuts to the heart of the Islamic understanding of wisdom: it is not intellectual cleverness but the foresight to subordinate immediate desires to long-term spiritual accounting.

The chapter also addresses tafakkur — deep reflection on the creation of Allah, the verses of the Quran, one's own spiritual states, and the realities of the Hereafter. The Prophet ﷺ said: 'An hour of reflection is better than a year of (supererogatory) worship.' (This narration has been discussed by scholars regarding its chain, but its meaning is supported by numerous authentic hadiths emphasizing reflection.) The Quran repeatedly calls upon its readers to 'think', 'reflect', 'consider', 'observe' — the cognitive engagement with divine reality is itself a form of worship: 'Indeed, in the creation of the heavens and the earth and the alternation of night and day are signs for those of understanding — those who remember Allah while standing or sitting or lying on their sides and give thought to the creation of the heavens and the earth' (Quran 3:190-191).

Practically, al-Nawawi recommends that the believer adopt a daily practice of evening muhasabah: before sleeping, reviewing the day's actions, acknowledging what was good (with gratitude), acknowledging what was sinful (with tawbah), and noting what was neglected (with a resolve to restore). This simple discipline, maintained consistently, gradually transforms the believer's relationship with their own moral life — moving them from reactive (confessing sins after committing many) to proactive (catching moral drift early and correcting it before it deepens).
